article.title: Skyroot Aerospace Closes $60 Million Round and Becomes India's First Space-Tech Unicorn Weeks Before Vikram-1's Maiden Orbital Launch
article.summary: Hyderabad-based Skyroot raised $60 million co-led by Sherpalo Ventures and GIC at a $1.1 billion pre-money valuation, capping the round just weeks before its three-stage Vikram-1 rocket attempts India's first private orbital launch from Sriharikota.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Skyroot Aerospace has raised about $60 million at a $1.1 billion pre-money valuation, becoming India's first space-tech unicorn ahead of the maiden orbital flight of its Vikram-1 rocket, [TechCrunch reported](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/07/indias-first-space-tech-unicorn-emerges-as-skyroot-gears-up-for-orbital-launch/) on May 7, 2026. The Hyderabad startup, founded in 2018 by former Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) engineers Pawan Kumar Chandana and Naga Bharath Daka, expects to launch Vikram-1 from the Satish Dhawan Space Centre at Sriharikota in [June 2026](https://thenextweb.com/news/skyroot-india-space-tech-unicorn-gic-blackrock).

## What We Know

The round was co-led by Sherpalo Ventures and Singapore's sovereign wealth fund GIC, [according to TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/07/indias-first-space-tech-unicorn-emerges-as-skyroot-gears-up-for-orbital-launch/). The deal is structured as roughly $50 million in primary equity from Sherpalo and GIC plus around $10 million in structured debt managed by funds affiliated with BlackRock. [The Tribune India](https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/india/skyroot-aerospace-becomes-indias-first-spacetech-unicorn-receives-usd-60-million-funding/) lists Playbook Partners, Arkam Ventures, the Shanghvi Family Office, and the founders of Greenko Group among other participating investors.

The new valuation more than doubles the roughly $500 million pre-money mark Skyroot secured in its 2023 financing round, [per TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/07/indias-first-space-tech-unicorn-emerges-as-skyroot-gears-up-for-orbital-launch/). As part of the deal, Sherpalo founder and Alphabet board member Ram Shriram is joining Skyroot's board, [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/07/indias-first-space-tech-unicorn-emerges-as-skyroot-gears-up-for-orbital-launch/) and [The Tribune India](https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/india/skyroot-aerospace-becomes-indias-first-spacetech-unicorn-receives-usd-60-million-funding/) confirm.

Vikram-1, which The Next Web [describes](https://thenextweb.com/news/skyroot-india-space-tech-unicorn-gic-blackrock) as a three-stage solid-fuelled rocket targeting a 500-kilometre sun-synchronous orbit, will carry small satellites to low Earth orbit on its debut flight. The Tribune India [reports](https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/india/skyroot-aerospace-becomes-indias-first-spacetech-unicorn-receives-usd-60-million-funding/) that the vehicle is built with an entirely carbon-composite airframe and powered by 3D-printed engines developed in-house. TechCrunch [notes](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/07/indias-first-space-tech-unicorn-emerges-as-skyroot-gears-up-for-orbital-launch/) that Skyroot is building small satellite launchers broadly comparable in scale to those built by U.S. operators Rocket Lab and Firefly Aerospace.

"We at Skyroot are excited about the upcoming Vikram-1 launch, India's first private orbital rocket, marking a significant milestone both for India and the global space sector," Chandana, the company's co-founder and chief executive, [told The Tribune India](https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/india/skyroot-aerospace-becomes-indias-first-spacetech-unicorn-receives-usd-60-million-funding/).

The round also marks Skyroot as the fourth Indian startup to enter the unicorn club in 2026, [The Tribune India](https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/india/skyroot-aerospace-becomes-indias-first-spacetech-unicorn-receives-usd-60-million-funding/) notes, and the first from the country's space-tech sector. Skyroot's previous milestone, Mission Prarambh in November 2022, made it the first private Indian company to send a rocket past the Karman line when its sub-orbital Vikram-S vehicle lifted off from Sriharikota, [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/skyroot-india-space-tech-unicorn-gic-blackrock) reports.

## What We Don't Know

The sources reviewed disagree on Vikram-1's exact payload capacity to low Earth orbit: [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/07/indias-first-space-tech-unicorn-emerges-as-skyroot-gears-up-for-orbital-launch/) and [The Tribune India](https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/india/skyroot-aerospace-becomes-indias-first-spacetech-unicorn-receives-usd-60-million-funding/) cite up to 350 kilograms, while [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/skyroot-india-space-tech-unicorn-gic-blackrock) cites up to 480 kilograms; the company has not publicly reconciled the figures. The Vikram-1 launch date inside the June 2026 window has not been set publicly. Capital allocation between the Vikram-1 launch campaign, expanded manufacturing, and development of the larger Vikram-2 vehicle has not been disclosed in dollar terms.

## Analysis

India's Space Policy of 2023 explicitly opened launch and satellite-services sectors to private operators, [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/skyroot-india-space-tech-unicorn-gic-blackrock) notes, and Skyroot's unicorn round is the largest validation to date of that policy turn at venture stage. The pricing is notable: a $1.1 billion valuation before a single orbital flight implies Skyroot's backers are underwriting both successful first-flight execution and a credible path to a regular launch cadence in a market historically dominated by ISRO's state-run vehicles. Whether that bet pays off will be tested first at Sriharikota in the weeks ahead.
